name: Inhibitory Control Threshold Model with Resource Depletion
description: A deterministic threshold-comparison model where eating occurs when hunger-amplified, affect-adjusted food-cue salience exceeds a dynamically computed self-regulation threshold. The threshold integrates three modular executive function components (inhibitory control, working memory-supported goal activation, cognitive flexibility) following the unity/diversity EF framework from Miyake et al. Inhibitory control is the primary contributor and depletes with repeated food-cue exposure, capturing ego-depletion-adjacent dynamics. Working memory load from environmental complexity degrades dietary goal activation. Cognitive flexibility tracks the agent's ability to shift strategy when the food environment changes. Affective state modulates both the threshold (positive affect helps) and effective salience (negative affect amplifies craving).

variables.name: inhibitory_control
variables.description: Current available inhibitory control capacity; depletes with food-cue exposure, recovers without

variables.name: working_memory_load
variables.description: Current working memory occupation (0=free, 1=fully loaded); higher load = fewer resources for dietary goals

variables.name: cognitive_flexibility
variables.description: Ability to shift eating strategy when context changes; improves with successful adaptation, degrades with perseveration

variables.name: food_cue_salience
variables.description: Raw perceptual salience of nearest food cue, derived from palatability and proximity

variables.name: affect_adjusted_salience
variables.description: Salience after affective modulation; negative affect amplifies it

variables.name: hunger
variables.description: Homeostatic hunger signal, increases each step, decreases on eating

variables.name: affective_state
variables.description: Emotional valence; positive = good mood, negative = stress/negative affect

variables.name: goal_activation
variables.description: Strength of dietary restraint goal maintained in working memory

variables.name: effective_threshold
variables.description: Computed self-regulation threshold that salience must exceed to trigger eating

variables.name: previous_decision
variables.description: The decision made on the previous step ('eat' or 'restrain'), used for flexibility tracking

variables.name: previous_food_configuration
variables.description: Hash or summary of food positions last step to detect environment changes for flexibility tracking

parameters.name: ic_depletion_per_food_encounter
parameters.source: Estimated from repeated inhibition cost (ScienceDirect / Appetite, 2017)

parameters.name: ic_recovery_rate
parameters.source: Half the depletion rate; asymmetric recovery (MDPI / Nutrients, 2024)

parameters.name: weight_of_ic_in_threshold
parameters.source: Primary EF component for eating regulation (Wiley / Eating Disorders, 2023)

parameters.name: weight_of_wm_goal_in_threshold
parameters.source: Secondary EF contribution via goal maintenance (PMC, 2017)

parameters.name: cognitive_flexibility_improvement
parameters.source: CF improves with successful strategy shifts (ScienceDirect / Appetite, 2017)

rules.description: Compute raw food-cue salience S from nearest food's palatability and distance
rules.pseudocode:
foods = perception.resources.food
if foods:
    nearest = min(foods, key=lambda f: abs(f.x - perception.x) + abs(f.y - perception.y))
    dist = abs(nearest.x - perception.x) + abs(nearest.y - perception.y)
    S = nearest.palatability / (1.0 + dist)
else:
    S = 0.0

rules.description: Compute effective salience S_eff by amplifying raw salience with hunger
Compute affect-adjusted salience S_star: negative affect amplifies craving
Compute goal activation G from baseline and available working memory
Compute effective self-regulation threshold Theta from EF components and affect
rules.pseudocode: Theta = w_IC * IC + w_WM * G + w_CF * CF + w_aff * max(0.0, E_aff)

rules.description: Eat/restrain decision: compare affect-adjusted salience against threshold
Inhibitory control depletion when food cues present, recovery when absent
Hunger dynamics: increase each step, decrease on eating
Affective state dynamics: eating low-palatability food improves affect, high-palatability causes guilt-like negative shift, small random drift
Working memory load: increases when multiple food items visible (choice conflict), decays otherwise
Cognitive flexibility: improves when agent switches strategy after food config changes, degrades with perseveration

decision_logic.description: Deterministic threshold-based decision: compute food-cue salience (modulated by hunger and affect), compute self-regulation threshold (from IC, WM-supported goal, CF, affect), compare salience to threshold. If salience exceeds threshold, approach or eat food. Otherwise, restrain by staying or moving away. All three EF components modularly contribute to the threshold.
pseudocode: # 1. Update internal states from last step outcome
Compute S using rule R1 (nearest food palatability / (1 + distance))
Compute S_eff = S * (1 + kappa * H)  # rule R2
Compute S_star = S_eff * (1 + |min(0, E_aff)|)  # rule R3
Theta = w_IC * IC + w_WM * G + w_CF * CF + w_aff * max(0, E_aff)  # rule R5
action = random_exploration_move()  # move in random direction

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception.resources.food  # list of {x, y, type, palatability}
perception_to_variables.food_palatability: each food item's .palatability (float 0.1–1.0) maps to salience; high palatability = high salience
perception_to_variables.last_food_palatability: stored from the food item consumed (tracked internally from nearest food at time of eat action)

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect reward (1.0); internal affective/threshold dynamics use palatability to differentiate healthy vs indulgent outcomes

expected_behaviors.description: Inhibitory control gradually depletes when agent stays near food, eventually causing salience to exceed threshold and triggering eating
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: Place agent near high-palatability food for 15+ steps without eating → assert IC decreases below 0.3 and decision eventually switches to 'eat'

expected_behaviors.description: High hunger amplifies effective salience, making it easier to exceed threshold
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: Set H=0.9 near medium-palatability food → assert S_eff > Theta; Set H=0.1 → assert S_eff < Theta

expected_behaviors.description: Working memory load from multiple visible food items degrades goal activation, lowering threshold
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: Place 4 food items within distance 5 of agent → assert WM increases and G decreases, lowering Theta compared to 1 food item scenario

expected_behaviors.description: Negative affective state amplifies food-cue salience, increasing likelihood of eating
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: Set E_aff = -0.8 vs E_aff = 0.5 with same food config → assert S_star is higher when E_aff is negative, leading to more eat decisions

expected_behaviors.description: Cognitive flexibility improves when agent successfully shifts strategy after food environment changes
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: Change food positions between steps → agent switches from 'eat' to 'restrain' → assert CF increases by cf_improve_rate

expected_behaviors.description: IC recovers when agent moves away from all food items
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: Deplete IC to 0.2, then ensure no food within perception for 10 steps → assert IC rises toward IC_0

references: CORE / Frontiers in Psychology (2016) — Impulsivity and Inhibitory Control Deficits Are Associated with Obesity
ScienceDirect / Appetite (2017) — Executive functions and the self-regulation of eating behavior: A review
MDPI / Nutrients (2024) — Self-Regulation in Eating Behaviors: The Role of Executive Function in Response to Food Stimuli
Springer / Reviews in Endocrine and Metabolic Disorders (2021) — Neurobiological regulation of eating behavior
Wiley / International Journal of Eating Disorders (2023) — Executive functions and eating behavior
PMC (2017) — Executive functioning and dietary intake
